perturbation.original_text: The Confidant must take all reasonable precautions to avoid unauthorised disclosure and protect against unauthorised use o f the Confidential Information , such precautions to be at least as stringent as those used by the Confidant for the protection of the Confidant ’s own confidential information and always in accordance with the Privacy Act 1988 .
perturbation.changed_text: The Confidant should endeavour to take precautions to avoid unauthorised disclosure and protect against unauthorised use of the Confidential Information, aiming for precautions similar to those used for the Confidant’s own confidential information.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'must take all reasonable precautions' with 'should endeavour to take precautions' and removing 'always in accordance with the Privacy Act 1988' weakens the obligation and introduces ambiguity, potentially allowing for insufficient data protection measures. Aiming for precautions 'similar to' their own allows for subjective interpretation.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Australian Privacy Act 1988 - Australian Privacy Principle 11.1
perturbation.law_explanation: APP 11.1 mandates that an APP entity must take active steps to protect personal information it holds from misuse, interference and loss, and from unauthorised access, modification or disclosure. The modified text weakens the requirement to 'endeavour' and doesn't explicitly require compliance with the Privacy Act, potentially leading to insufficient protection of personal information which would be a violation of the law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Australian Privacy Principles quick reference | OAIC We use cookies on this site We use cookies to analyse traffic and to improve your browsing experience on our website. To find out more, read our privacy policy . Close Skip to main content Australian Privacy Principles quick reference Download the poster (PDF, 614 KB) Principle Title Purpose APP 1 Open and transparent management of personal information Ensures that APP entities manage personal information in an open and transparent way. This includes having a clearly expressed and up to date APP privacy policy . APP 2 Anonymity and pseudonymity Requires APP entities to give individuals the option of not identifying themselves, or of using a pseudonym. Limited exceptions apply. APP 3 Collection of solicited personal information Outlines when an APP entity can collect personal information that is solicited. It applies higher standards to the collection of sensitive information . APP 4 Dealing with unsolicited personal information Outlines how APP entities must deal with unsolicited personal information. APP 5 Notification of the collection of personal information Outlines when and in what circumstances an APP entity that collects personal information must tell an individual about certain matters. APP 6 Use or disclosure of personal information Outlines the circumstances in which an APP entity may use or disclose personal information that it holds. APP 7 Direct marketing An organisation may only use or disclose personal information for direct marketing purposes if certain conditions are met. APP 8 Cross-border disclosure of personal information Outlines the steps an APP entity must take to protect personal information before it is disclosed overseas. APP 9 Adoption, use or disclosure of government related identifiers Outlines the limited circumstances when an organisation may adopt a government related identifier of an individual as its own identifier, or use or disclose a government related identifier of an individual. APP 10 Quality of personal information An APP entity must take reasonable steps to ensure the personal information it collects is accurate, up to date and complete. An entity must also take reasonable steps to ensure the personal information it uses or discloses is accurate, up to date, complete and relevant, having regard to the purpose of the use or disclosure. APP 11 Security of personal information An APP entity must take reasonable steps to protect personal information it holds from misuse, interference and loss, and from unauthorised access, modification or disclosure. An entity has obligations to destroy or de-identify personal information in certain circumstances. APP 12 Access to personal information Outlines an APP entity’s obligations when an individual requests to be given access to personal information held about them by the entity. This includes a requirement to provide access unless a specific exception applies. APP 13 Correction of personal information Outlines an APP entity’s obligations in relation to correcting the personal information it holds about individuals. perturbation.justification: The changed text weakens the original obligation to protect confidential information. The original text states the 'Confidant must take all reasonable precautions...always in accordance with the Privacy Act 1988', whereas the modified text states 'The Confidant should endeavour to take precautions...aiming for precautions similar to those used for the Confidant’s own confidential information.' This introduces ambiguity and weakens the obligation. The Law Explanation states APP 11.1 mandates that an APP entity must take active steps to protect personal information it holds from misuse, interference and loss, and from unauthorised access, modification or disclosure. Scraped Law Snippet 2 lists APP 11 as Security of personal information: An APP entity must take reasonable steps to protect personal information it holds from misuse, interference and loss, and from unauthorised access, modification or disclosure. Because the changed text weakens this requirement, a contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: At the conclusion of the Datathon the Confi dant must permanently delete all data provided by NostraData .
perturbation.changed_text: At the conclusion of the Datathon the Confidant should delete the data provided by NostraData.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'must permanently delete' to 'should delete' weakens the legal obligation. 'Should' introduces ambiguity and allows for discretion, potentially resulting in the data not being deleted, retained indefinitely, or improperly deleted, increasing the risk of unauthorized access.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Australian Privacy Act 1988 - Australian Privacy Principle 11.2
perturbation.law_explanation: APP 11.2 mandates that an APP entity must take reasonable steps to destroy or de-identify personal information if it is no longer needed for any purpose for which the information may be used or disclosed under the APPs. The modified text weakens the 'must' requirement by using 'should' which does not guarantee the data deletion as required under the Privacy Act.

perturbation.justification: The original text uses 'must permanently delete', imposing a strict obligation. The changed text uses 'should delete', weakening this obligation. The Law Explanation states that APP 11.2 mandates an APP entity to take reasonable steps to destroy or de-identify personal information. The changed text does not guarantee data deletion, potentially violating APP 11.2. Scraped Law Snippet 2 lists APP 11 regarding security of personal information and an entity's obligation to destroy or de-identify personal information in certain circumstances.

perturbation.original_text: The Confidant must not attempt to re-identify any personal information that has been de -identified in the Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: The Confidant will use its best efforts not to re-identify any personal information that has been de-identified in the Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'must not' to 'will use its best efforts not to' weakens the prohibition. 'Best efforts' is subjective and open to interpretation, potentially allowing for scenarios where re-identification is attempted without genuine effort to prevent it.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Australian Privacy Act 1988 - Australian Privacy Principle 11.1
perturbation.law_explanation: APP 11.1 requires organizations to protect personal information from misuse, interference, loss, and unauthorized access or disclosure. Attempting to re-identify de-identified information presents a risk of violating privacy principles. The original text provides a clear legal obligation while the modified text uses ambiguous language weakening the obligation and allowing room for interpretation.

perturbation.justification: The original text imposes a strict prohibition ('must not attempt'). The changed text weakens this prohibition by introducing a 'best efforts' standard. According to the Perturbation Explanation, 'Best efforts' is subjective and open to interpretation. This directly contradicts the intent of APP 11.1, as outlined in the Law Explanation, which requires organizations to protect personal information. Scraped Law Snippet 2, referencing APP 11, states that an entity 'must take reasonable steps to protect personal information it holds from misuse, interference and loss, and from unauthorized access, modification or disclosure.' Replacing 'must not' with 'best efforts' weakens the obligation to protect personal information, creating a contradiction of APP 11. The original strict obligation is now replaced by a weaker, subjective obligation, thereby failing to meet the standard of 'reasonable steps' required by APP 11.

perturbation.original_text: Following any request by NostraData , the Confidant must immediately provide to NostraData all Confidential Information and certify that no Confidential Information is retained by the Confidant . The obligation to return Confidential Information includ es the obligation to return any and all Confidential Information in physical, electronic or other tangible form.
perturbation.changed_text: Following a reasonable request by NostraData, the Confidant should attempt to provide to NostraData the Confidential Information and provide assurance that no Confidential Information is knowingly retained by the Confidant. The obligation to return Confidential Information includes the obligation to return Confidential Information in tangible form.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'any request' with 'a reasonable request' makes the trigger for the obligation subjective. Replacing 'must immediately provide' with 'should attempt to provide' weakens the obligation. Replacing 'certify that no Confidential Information is retained' with 'provide assurance that no Confidential Information is knowingly retained' introduces ambiguity as it allows for unintentional retention. Removing 'physical, electronic' limits the scope of what must be returned.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Australian Privacy Act 1988 - Australian Privacy Principle 11.2
perturbation.law_explanation: APP 11.2 requires entities to take reasonable steps to destroy or de-identify personal information once it's no longer needed for its intended purpose. Retaining data after a request would contradict this principle. The altered text weakens the obligation to return the information after a reasonable request instead of an immediate response as well as not accounting for all forms of confidential information to be returned. By including 'knowingly' in the context of retaining the information also provides an avenue to not be held responsible for failure to follow the law.

perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the obligation to return confidential information, potentially leading to non-compliance with APP 11 regarding the security of personal information. The original text required immediate provision of all confidential information and certification of no retention. The changed text introduces subjectivity ('reasonable request'), weakens the obligation ('should attempt'), and creates ambiguity about retention ('knowingly retained'). This contradicts APP 11's requirement to take reasonable steps to protect personal information from misuse and unauthorized access, as the weakened obligation and ambiguity could allow for unintended retention or loss of confidential information after a request, and failure to account for all formats as stipulated in the original text. The scraped law snippet 2 mentions 'An entity has obligations to destroy or de-identify personal information in certain circumstances,' which aligns with the intention of the original text but is undermined by the changes.
